cross_system.title: Cross-system signal: credit_card flow softening ahead of stock
cross_system.body: Credit card spending is slowing down, which usually happens before credit card loans start shrinking. When people spend less on their cards, they typically pay down their balances over the next few months, causing total credit card debt to drop. This gives banks a few months' head start to see what's coming.
cross_system.implication: Banks should prepare for lower credit card loan growth and adjust their lending plans before the slowdown hits their loan books.
chain: People are spending less money on their credit cards
Lower spending usually leads to people paying down their card balances over the next few months
Banks can spot this early and adjust their credit card lending before loan growth actually slows

sibc.title: Co-lending & warehouse-financing infrastructure
sibc.body: Small business credit is growing fast at 30.09% this year and now makes up 23.12% of all business lending. The government's UDYAM registration and GST data are making tiny businesses easier to check for creditworthiness. But banks struggle to reach these small borrowers directly because there are so many of them.
sibc.implication: Build partnerships with finance companies that can find and assess small borrowers, then either lend together or provide funding to those finance companies.
chain: Government systems make small businesses easier to track and verify
Small business lending grows 30% but banks can't reach all these borrowers directly
Banks partner with finance companies who can find small borrowers and either share the lending or fund the finance company
sibc._driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: Gold loan market entry / deepening for banks
sibc.body: Banks are growing gold loans by 121% year-over-year and have been growing for 8 straight periods. They're taking customers away from NBFCs because gold prices shot up from 2023 to 2026, making gold-backed loans more attractive to borrowers. Banks can offer better rates and service because they have local branches to check gold quality and store it safely.
sibc.implication: Banks with many branches should quickly expand their gold loan business to grab market share while gold prices stay high.
chain: Gold prices rose sharply from 2023 to 2026
Higher gold prices let people borrow more money against their gold jewelry
Banks beat NBFCs because they can check and store gold at local branches

sibc.title: MSME origination infrastructure investment window
sibc.body: Small business lending is exploding at 30% annual growth, and these tiny businesses now represent 23% of all MSME lending. Banks that built digital systems to assess small businesses using government registration data and GST records are winning big. Banks without these systems have about 2-3 years to catch up before the opportunity window closes.
sibc.implication: Banks should immediately invest in digital underwriting systems that can analyze UDYAM registrations and GST data to safely lend to small businesses.
chain: Government digitized small business registration and made tax data available
Banks can now assess creditworthiness of tiny businesses they couldn't lend to before
Early-moving banks are capturing fast-growing market share while others get left behind
sibc._driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: PLI anchor-supplier chain finance
sibc.body: The government's PLI scheme is paying big manufacturers to boost production. These companies are expanding fast and need more suppliers. Basic metals lending grew 17%, chemicals grew 20%, and engineering grew 35%. Banks can lend to these suppliers more safely because they have contracts with large established companies that guarantee payment.
sibc.implication: Banks should offer supply chain finance products like invoice discounting to PLI suppliers instead of regular unsecured business loans.
chain: Government pays manufacturers to increase production
Manufacturers expand and sign contracts with more suppliers
Banks can safely lend to suppliers because big companies back the payments

sibc.title: PSLC market depth from PSL reclassification surplus
sibc.body: RBI changed the rules in October 2024 to let more housing loans count toward banks' required priority sector lending. This instantly gave some banks extra PSL credit without doing any new lending - housing PSL lending jumped 39.94% year-over-year. Banks with this surprise surplus can now sell PSL certificates to banks that are behind on their targets.
sibc.implication: If your bank got extra PSL credit from the rule change, sell certificates to banks missing their targets - if you're behind on PSL goals, buy certificates from banks with surplus.
chain: RBI rule change let more housing loans count for required priority lending
Some banks suddenly had extra PSL credit without making new loans
Banks with surplus can sell certificates to banks that need to hit their targets
sibc._driver: RBI PSL housing loan ceiling revision (Oct 2024)

sibc.title: Renewable energy project finance — next capex wave
sibc.body: Traditional infrastructure lending is winding down — some sectors dropped 26% and 44% this year while others grew 74% and 21%, showing the boom-bust cycle is ending. Banks spent the last few years building teams that know how to finance big, long-term projects like roads and power plants. Government renewable energy targets are creating a new pipeline of solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects that need the same type of financing.
sibc.implication: Banks should move their project finance teams from traditional infrastructure to renewable energy deals.
chain: Traditional infrastructure projects are finishing their building phase
Banks now have experienced teams who know how to finance large projects
Government renewable targets create new solar and wind projects that need the same financing skills
sibc._via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: Services sector credit entry / scale-up
sibc.body: Services sector credit is growing at 18.58% per year, much faster than other sectors, and now makes up 28.21% of all bank lending. NBFCs that specialize in services lending are growing even faster at 16.68% to 57.1% as banks start lending to them again after regulatory changes.
sibc.implication: Banks should either build direct lending teams for services companies or increase their lending to NBFCs that already serve this fast-growing market.
chain: Services companies need more credit and are growing fast at 18.58% per year
NBFCs that lend to services are recovering and growing at 16-57% as banks lend to them again
Banks can grow by either lending directly to services companies or by lending more money to these specialized NBFCs
sibc._driver: NBFC credit channel recovery post risk-weight cycle

sibc.title: EV-specific vehicle credit products
sibc.body: RETIRED at first FOUNDATION (2026-06-10). No external source verified for EV share of vehicle loans or EV-specific credit risk differentiation. No L1 signal separating EV from ICE vehicle loans. Returns only when EV share of vehicle loans is measurable in L1 (e.g., VAHAN registration data ingestion).
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.

sibc.title: Infrastructure repayment wave with insufficient new origination pipeline
sibc.body: Banks over-indexed to traditional infrastructure credit face a structural squeeze: the FY22-24 project book moves to repayment (compressing balances) while the next-cycle pipeline (renewables, data centres) is slower to ramp. Banks without next-cycle project finance capability face credit portfolio contraction.
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: As a project cohort moves from construction (drawdown) to operations (repayment), net credit balance declines unless new origination offsets it — suppressing growth even while existing books remain large.
This is being driven by Infrastructure supercycle completing (FY22–24).
sibc._via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: CIC reporting compliance gaps creating off-balance-sheet credit risk
sibc.body: RBI Digital Lending Guidelines (2022/2025) require BNPL and digital credit to report to Credit Information Companies. However, compliance may be incomplete among newer digital lenders operating under first-loss guarantee structures or co-lending arrangements where the regulated entity's on-book exposure is partially shielded. If reporting gaps exist, the true unsecured credit stress is understated in bank balance sheets and in SIBC data.
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Higher risk weights raise the regulatory capital cost per unit of exposure, compressing origination economics for unsecured retail products until reversed.
This is being driven by RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023).
sibc._driver: RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023)
sibc._via: Regulatory capital weight → unsecured origination economics

atm_pos.title: Credit card penetration deepening — PSB share gap is the opening
atm_pos.body: Credit cards are growing 8.15% yearly with 119 million total cards. Public sector banks have only 24% of credit cards but 64% of debit cards, meaning they have millions of existing customers who could get credit cards but don't. Converting existing customers costs much less than finding new ones.
atm_pos.implication: Public sector banks should offer credit card upgrades to their existing debit card customers since they already know and trust these customers.
chain: Public sector banks have way more debit card customers than credit card customers
Their existing customers already trust them and have working bank accounts
Converting existing customers to credit cards costs much less than finding brand new customers

atm_pos.title: Rural cash distribution via Micro ATM / Business Correspondent networks
atm_pos.body: Micro ATMs dropped 7.17% but there are still 1.37 million of them serving rural areas. Rural people still need cash but these areas can't support the cost of full ATMs or bank branches. This creates a gap that smaller, cheaper micro ATMs can fill.
atm_pos.implication: Banks should expand their business correspondent networks with micro ATM devices to serve rural cash demand at lower cost than building full branches.
chain: Rural areas still need cash access but full ATMs cost too much to install and maintain
Micro ATMs can provide basic cash services at much lower cost than full ATMs or branches
Banks can serve this rural market by partnering with local business correspondents who operate micro ATMs

atm_pos.title: Embedded transit and tap-to-pay payment products
atm_pos.body: Credit card spending in the 'other' category grew 493% in value and 300% in volume this year. This massive jump comes from new payment systems that let people use credit cards for metro rides, bus fares, and quick tap payments at stores. People are switching from cash and debit cards to credit cards for these everyday purchases.
atm_pos.implication: Banks should rush to get their credit cards working with these new transit and tap-payment systems before competitors grab the customers.
chain: New payment systems are being built that accept credit cards for metro rides and quick store payments
People are trying these systems and switching from cash to credit cards for daily purchases
Banks that connect their cards to these systems first will capture these new spending habits
atm_pos._driver: Embedded card payment infrastructure rollout (transit NFC or RuPay-credit-on-UPI)
atm_pos._via: Embedded payment rails → new card-spend surface

atm_pos.title: Credit card market concentrated in top-5 private banks
atm_pos.body: The top-5 private banks control approximately 71% of credit card outstanding. The largest single issuer holds ~20% of the market. This concentration creates systemic fragility — as demonstrated when a single payment bank's exit caused a market-wide debit card step-down. Similar disruption from a major credit card issuer would have proportionally larger effects.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.

atm_pos.title: Debit card base structural decline reducing cash access for underbanked
atm_pos.body: The debit card base is contracting through payment bank exits and lifecycle purges. This reduces the potential transaction base for populations that depend on debit cards for cash access and digital payments — particularly lower-income and rural segments without credit cards or reliable UPI-linked accounts.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Regulatory wind-down of a major issuer forces cancellation of its entire card portfolio, producing a single-period step-down in the stock concentrated in that issuer's lender category.
This is being driven by RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026).
atm_pos._driver: RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026)

atm_pos.title: Payment acceptance bifurcation: POS contracting while UPI grows
atm_pos.body: POS terminal network contraction while UPI QR expands creates an acceptance gap: merchants who drop POS terminals cannot accept international cards, EMI transactions, premium credit card features, or POS-authenticated payments. Customers without UPI-linked accounts face reduced options at merchants who have switched fully to UPI.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Zero MDR on UPI makes QR acceptance structurally cheaper than card POS (no hardware, no per-txn fee), driving QR deployment while suppressing POS-terminal investment. Polarity is per-target: + for QR, − for POS.
This is being driven by Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI).
atm_pos._driver: Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI)
